name: IGF-1 LR3
summary: IGF-1 LR3 is a synthetic 83-amino-acid analog of insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) engineered for greatly increased stability and potency. It is not a growth hormone secretagogue: it acts downstream of GH as a direct IGF-1 receptor agonist, and is grouped under the GH-axis category because IGF-1 is the principal effector hormone of that axis. It is an investigational research chemical with no human therapeutic approval.
mechanism:
Native IGF-1 is a 70-amino-acid peptide and the main downstream mediator of growth hormone (GH): GH stimulates hepatic and local IGF-1 production, and IGF-1 then drives much of GH's anabolic and growth-promoting signaling. IGF-1 LR3 is a recombinant analog with two engineered changes: an arginine substituted for glutamic acid at position 3 ('R3'), and a 13-amino-acid N-terminal extension ('Long'), giving 83 residues total.

These modifications dramatically lower its affinity for the IGF binding proteins (IGFBPs) that normally sequester and clear circulating IGF-1. As a result a far greater fraction of IGF-1 LR3 stays free and available to bind the IGF-1 receptor, a tyrosine-kinase receptor that activates the PI3K/Akt and Ras/MAPK pathways to promote cell growth, proliferation, protein synthesis, and survival. Because the IGF-1 receptor is highly homologous to the insulin receptor, IGF-1 also exerts insulin-like effects, including lowering blood glucose. Reduced IGFBP binding plus slower renal clearance give IGF-1 LR3 an in vivo half-life roughly 20-30x longer than free native IGF-1 and an estimated ~3-fold greater biological potency.
benefits: Direct, sustained activation of the IGF-1 receptor (acts downstream of GH rather than stimulating GH release)
Studied in research models for skeletal muscle hypertrophy and satellite cell proliferation
Investigated for tissue repair, bone biology, and neuroprotection in preclinical work
Much longer-acting and more potent than native IGF-1 due to minimal IGFBP binding
Widely used as a laboratory reagent for cell culture and sustained IGF-1R signaling studies
risks: Highly investigational: sold as a research chemical, not FDA-approved or approved for any human use
Hypoglycemia is a primary acute danger, since IGF-1 has insulin-like glucose-lowering activity and the long half-life can cause prolonged, hard-to-reverse blood sugar drops

Theoretical and biologically plausible cancer risk: IGF-1 receptor signaling promotes cell proliferation and survival, and elevated IGF-1 levels are epidemiologically associated with prostate, breast, and colon cancers; sustained exogenous IGF-1R activation may accelerate growth of existing or undiagnosed tumors
Can drive non-selective growth of internal organs (organomegaly) and other tissues at high or chronic exposure
Long half-life (~20-30 hours) means effects and adverse events cannot be quickly stopped once injected
No established human dosing, purity, or long-term safety data; research-chemical sourcing carries contamination and mislabeling risk
Prohibited in sport: IGF-1 and its analogs are banned by WADA at all times
sideEffects: Hypoglycemia: shakiness, sweating, dizziness, confusion, palpitations, and in severe cases loss of consciousness
Injection-site redness, swelling, pain, or lipohypertrophy
Fluid retention, joint aches, or jaw/limb discomfort (IGF/GH-axis effects)
Reported numbness or tingling in extremities

dosage.notes: Educational reference only, not a protocol or medical advice. Research-context figures commonly cited fall in the 10-100 mcg/day range, with users often eating carbohydrate around dosing because of the hypoglycemia risk. Because IGF-1 LR3 has a long half-life and potent insulin-like activity, even small overestimates of dose can cause prolonged low blood sugar. No validated human therapeutic dose exists.
dosage.timing: Where dosed, it is typically given once daily, frequently within ~30-60 minutes post-workout on training days so the injection coincides with a planned carbohydrate-containing meal and exercise-related glucose uptake, both of which help blunt the hypoglycemia risk. Some research-context protocols split the daily amount or dose only on active/training days. The long ~20-30 hour half-life means a single daily injection sustains exposure; there is no validated human timing protocol.
dosage.maxDose: CAUTION: there is no clinically validated maximum and no human therapeutic dose. Research-chemical figures rarely exceed ~100 mcg/day, but higher or chronic exposure markedly increases the most serious IGF-1 risks - prolonged hard-to-reverse hypoglycemia, non-selective organ/tissue overgrowth (organomegaly), and theoretical cancer promotion via sustained IGF-1R activation. More is not safer; the ceiling here is a danger threshold, not a target.

steps.note: Conservative starting dose to assess tolerance and glucose response; inject post-workout with carbohydrate available and fast-acting glucose on hand.

Representative maintenance amount if well tolerated; still within the commonly cited 20-50 mcg/day band. Once daily, post-workout on training days. Educational reference only - not a protocol or medical advice.

frequency: Once daily when used in research contexts; some protocols dose only on training/active days
halfLife: Approximately 20-30 hours in vivo, roughly 20-30x longer than free native IGF-1 (which is cleared in minutes when unbound)
cycleLength: Short cycles are typically described in the research-chemical community (around 4 weeks), reflecting concern over IGF-1R desensitization and growth-related risks; no clinically validated cycle exists

reconstitution.notes: Example math: a 1 mg (1000 mcg) vial + 2 mL bacteriostatic water = 500 mcg/mL, so 20 mcg = 0.04 mL (4 units on a U-100 insulin syringe). IGF-1 LR3 is sometimes reconstituted with bacteriostatic water; some sources prefer acetic-acid-based diluents for stability. Swirl gently, do not shake.

storage: Store lyophilized vials frozen (-20C) and protect from light. After reconstitution, refrigerate at 2-8C and use within a few weeks; avoid freeze-thaw cycles of the reconstituted solution.
contraindications: Active, prior, or suspected cancer, or strong family/personal risk of IGF-driven malignancies (prostate, breast, colon)
Diabetes or any condition predisposing to unstable blood glucose (hypoglycemia risk)
Pediatric or adolescent use outside of clinical supervision (uncontrolled growth signaling)
Competitive athletes subject to anti-doping testing

interactions.note: CJC-1295 raises GH, which raises endogenous IGF-1; stacking with exogenous IGF-1 LR3 is redundant on the same pathway and compounds growth and hypoglycemia/organ-growth risks rather than adding a distinct benefit.
Ipamorelin is a GH secretagogue that increases IGF-1 indirectly; combining with direct IGF-1 LR3 layers two sources of IGF-1R activation and amplifies axis-related risks.
Tesamorelin (a GHRH analog) elevates GH and IGF-1; adding exogenous IGF-1 LR3 is overlapping/redundant on the GH-IGF-1 axis and increases cumulative growth and metabolic risk.
IGF-1 has insulin-like glucose-lowering activity; combining with insulin or insulin secretagogues sharply increases the risk of severe, prolonged hypoglycemia.
